content:
Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>A</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-07-12</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

PROGRESS SUMMARY CHECKPOINT
===========================
Write progress notes of what was done, what was learned, and what's next. This is a checkpoint to capture progress so far. The session is ongoing - you may receive more requests and tool executions after this summary. Write "next_steps" as the current trajectory of work (what's actively being worked on or coming up next), not as post-session future work. Always write at least a minimal summary explaining current progress, even if work is still in early stages, so that users see a summary output tied to each request.

Claude's Full Response to User:
查完了，有明確收穫：業界（OpenClaw，多 channel agent bridge，Telegram 整合最成熟的開源專案之一）的做法直接回答了這個問題——**把「會變動的 tool 進度」和「只會追加的回答文字」拆成兩條通道**，不要塞在同一個 draft 裡。

## 查到的重點

**1. Draft 的動畫語意是官方規格、無法繞過。** Telegram 官方文件明確定義：更新 live draft 時 client 「淡入新增字元，數量 = `max(0, strlen(new) - strlen(prev))`」，**規格完全沒有提供 mid-text / 頭部變動的更新方式**——就是純 append-only 模型。所以只要 tool 行在 draft 裡原地翻 emoji、插秒數，抖動是規格層面註定的，任何排版技巧都只能緩解。

**2. OpenClaw 的解法：tool 進度走獨立 status 訊息，不進 draft。** 他們的 `progress` 模式是「把 tool 進度放在一則可編輯的 status 訊息裡（用 `editMessageText` 原地更新），完成時清掉這則 status，最終答案走正常訊息送出」。關鍵洞察是：**普通訊息的 edit 是整則重繪、沒有字元淡入動畫**，所以 emoji 翻轉、秒數插入、行數增減都不會抖——會抖的只有 draft。他們還有 `commandText: "status"` 選項：只顯示狀態摘要、不顯示原始命令（跟你昨天選的「只留工具種類」same idea）。

**3. 我們用的 `sendMessageDraft` 是對的底層選擇**（Bot API 9.3 引入、9.5 起全面開放，比 editMessageText 輪詢更省 rate limit），問題只在我們把 mutable 內容混進了 append-only 通道。

## 對照我們 bridge 的建議

**方案 A（OpenClaw-style 拆分，建議）**：
- Draft 只放回答文字（+ 恆定 header）→ 純 append-only，淡入動畫完全順滑
- Tool 進度（emoji / 秒數 / thinking preview）→ 獨立一則小 status 訊息，`editMessageText` 原地更新（tool 事件頻率低，rate limit 壓力小）
- Final render 時刪掉 status 訊息（或編輯成摘要行），正式回覆照現在的路徑送
- 代價：每 turn 多一則暫時訊息 + 一次 delete；改動範圍中等（editNowInner 加一條 status 通道）

**方案 B（最小改動）**：維持單一 draft，但 draft 幀裡 tool 行凍結格式（`▸ Read` 不翻 emoji、不加秒數、不滑視窗），只有 final 訊息顯示 ✅/秒數。抖動只剩「加新行」的合法追加，但 tool 區夾在 body 下方，body 繼續長時 tool 行上方的內容仍在變（body 追加會把 statusTail 往後推——這其實還是 mid-text 變動，B 只能減輕不能根治）。

結論：**方案 B 治標，方案 A 才治本**——因為只要 tool 行和 body 共存於同一個 draft，body 追加本身就會把 tool 區推來推去。要動手做 A 的話說一聲（這個規模建議照流程走輕量設計再實作）。

Sources:
- [Telegram — AI features for bots（draft 動畫官方語意）](https://core.telegram.org/api/bots/ai)
- [OpenClaw — Streaming and chunking（progress 模式 / status draft 設計）](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/streaming)
- [OpenClaw issue #31061 — native sendMessageDraft streaming](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/31061)
- [OpenClaw issue #32148 — Bot API 9.5 sendMessageDraft](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/32148)
- [grammyjs/stream — LLM 輸出 streaming 到 Telegram](https://github.com/grammyjs/stream)

Respond in this XML format:
<summary>
  <request>[Short title capturing the user's request AND the substance of what was discussed/done]</request>
  <investigated>[What has been explored so far? What was examined?]</investigated>
  <learned>[What have you learned about how things work?]</learned>
  <completed>[What work has been completed so far? What has shipped or changed?]</completed>
  <next_steps>[What are you actively working on or planning to work on next in this session?]</next_steps>
  <notes>[Additional insights or observations about the current progress]</notes>
</summary>

IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this next PROGRESS SUMMARY - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the summary content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful summary content.

Thank you, this summary will be very useful for keeping track of our progress!

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

結論：**方案 B 治標，方案 A 才治本**——因為只要 tool 行和 body 共存於同一個 draft，body 追加本身就會把 tool 區推來推去。要動手做 A 的話說一聲（這個規模建議照流程走輕量設計再實作）。

content.text:
<summary>
  <request>Research Telegram bot draft message jitter issue and find industry best practices for handling tool progress updates during streaming responses</request>
  <investigated>Telegram Bot API draft message animation semantics, OpenClaw open-source project's streaming implementation, official Telegram AI features documentation, and how draft updates handle append-only vs mid-text changes</investigated>
  <learned>Telegram's `sendMessageDraft` has append-only animation semantics by design - new characters fade in smoothly at the end, but any mid-text changes (emoji updates, timestamp inserts, line reordering) cause visible jitter because the spec provides no mechanism for mid-text updates. OpenClaw solves this by separating concerns: mutable tool progress goes into independent status messages (updated via `editMessageText` which redraws without animation), while append-only answer text stays in the draft channel. The current bridge implementation mixes both in a single draft, causing tool status lines to shift as answer body grows above them.</learned>
  <completed>Completed research phase with clear findings. Identified root cause (mixing mutable and immutable content in append-only channel) and documented two solution approaches: Solution A (OpenClaw-style separation with independent status messages - recommended, requires moderate code changes) and Solution B (minimal changes, freeze tool format in draft - only reduces symptoms).</completed>
  <next_steps>Awaiting decision on which solution to implement. If Solution A is chosen, will proceed with lightweight design phase before implementation to add separate status message channel for tool progress tracking.</next_steps>
  <notes>The investigation directly answered the core question by finding real-world production implementation (OpenClaw) that solved the exact same problem. The `sendMessageDraft` API choice was correct; the issue is architectural (channel separation) not API-level. Solution A is recommended because it addresses the root cause - as long as tool lines and body text coexist in the same draft, body growth will push tool sections around causing jitter.</notes>
</summary>

  <outcome>"{\"type\":\"text\",\"file\":{\"filePath\":\"G:\\\\AI\\\\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\\\\src\\\\run-prompt.ts\",\"content\":\"    if (content === lastRendered) return;\\n    lastRendered = content;\\n    lastEditAt = Date.now();\\n    try {\\n      if (placeholder) {\\n        if (config.tgRichStreamEnabled) {\\n          const richEdited = await tryEditRichMessageDraft(ctx.api, chatId, placeholder.message_id, content);\\n          if (richEdited) return;\\n        }\\n        await ctx.api.editMessageText(chatId, placeholder.message_id, content,\\n          parseMode ? { parse_mode: parseMode } : {});\\n      }\\n    } catch {\\n      // Telegram returns 400 if content identical; ignore.\\n    }\\n  };\\n\\n  // 並發互斥：onUpdate（每 chunk）/ keepalive / recovery 多路觸發 editNow，\\n  // 而 lastEditAt 只在成功後更新——送出失敗期間時間閘門全開，並發呼叫會在\\n  // 「trySendDraft 失敗 → !placeholder → sendMessage」降級分支各自建立\\n  // placeholder（2026-07-11 roadmap 事故：1x 則相同訊息）。coalesce 讓同一\\n  // 時間最多一個 in-flight，中間的觸發合併成結束後補跑一次。\\n  const editNow = coalesceAsyncRunner(editNowInner, () => turnFinalized);\\n\\n  // Draft keepalive：draft 是 ~30s TTL ephemeral，且「chat 收到任何一般訊息」\\n  // 也會立即清掉。長 tool 執行無輸出、rate limit 早退、內容不變早退時都不會\\n  // 刷新 → draft 過期，使用者看到訊息消失、下一幀才重現。每 10s 檢查，距上次\\n  // 成功送出 ≥10s 就強制同 draftId 重送最新內容續命（最壞間隔 20s < 30s TTL）。\\n  const DRAFT_KEEPALIVE_MS = 10_000;\\n  const draftKeepalive: NodeJS.Timeout | null = useDraftMode\\n    ? setInterval(() => {\\n        if (turnFinalized || !useDraftMode || !draftId) return;\\n        if (rateLimitedUntil() > Date.now()) return;\\n        if (Date.now() - lastDraftSentAt < DRAFT_KEEPALIVE_MS) return;\\n        // sentinel 繞過 identical check 強制重送（\\\"\\\" 不行：全空 render 也是 \\\"\\\"，\\n        // \\\"Thinking…\\\" 階段的 keepalive 會被 identical check 吃掉）\\n        lastRendered = \\\"\\\\x00\\\";\\n        void editNow();\\n      }, DRAFT_KEEPALIVE_MS)\\n    : null;\\n\\n  const unregisterRecovery = isRelay || isSilentMeta ? () => {} : onRateLimitRecovery(() => {\\n    if (turnFinalized) {\\n      console.log(\\n        `[rate-limit] chat ${chatId} recovery callback suppressed — turn already finalized`\\n      );\\n      return;\\n    }\\n    throttledSnapshotLogged = false;\\n    console.log(\\n      `[rate-limit] chat ${chatId} resuming edits — pushing latest buffer (${(session.buffer || \\\"\\\").length} chars)`\\n    );\\n    lastRendered = \\\"\\\";\\n    return editNow();\\n  });\\n\\n  session.onUpdate = isRelay\\n    ? undefined\\n    : () => {\\n        const elapsed = Date.now() - lastEditAt;\\n        if (elapsed >= STREAM_EDIT_INTERVAL_MS) {\\n          editNow();\\n        } else if (!editTimer) {\\n          editTimer = setTimeout(() => {\\n            editTimer = null;\\n            editNow();\\n          }, STREAM_EDIT_INTERVAL_MS - elapsed);\\n        }\\n      };\\n\\n\\n  try {\\n    // Self-fusion: for goal high-stakes decisions, get alternative perspective\\n    if (mode.kind === \\\"user\\\" && !isMeta) {\\n      const gs = await goalStore.get(config.memoryDir, chatId).catch(() => null);\\n      if (gs?.status === \\\"active\\\" && text.length > 50) {\\n        const decisionSignals = /\\\\b(選|choose|pick|decide|recommend|architecture|刪除|delete|migrate|重構|refactor|方案|approach)\\\\b/i;\\n        if (decisionSignals.test(text)) {\\n          const { trioInfer } = await import(\\\"./trio-llm.js\\\");\\n          const altResp = await trioInfer(text.slice(0, 500), {\\n            system: \\\"You are a contrarian advisor. Give a brief alternative perspective or risk consideration for this decision. 2-3 sentences max.\\\",\\n            maxTokens: 150, temperature: 0.9, timeout: 8_000,\\n          });\\n          if (altResp.text && altResp.source !== \\\"none\\\") {\\n            const fusion = await synthesizeDualPath(text.slice(0, 200), \\\"（主模型即將回覆）\\\", altResp.text);\\n            if (fusion) {\\n              promptText = buildFusionContext(text.slice(0, 200), fusion) + \\\"\\\\n\\\" + promptText;\\n            } else {\\n              promptText = `[Alternative perspective (${altResp.source})]\\\\n${altResp.text}\\\\n[End]\\\\n\\\\n` + promptText;\\n            }\\n          }\\n        }\\n      }\\n    }\\n\\n    const { stopReason } = await session.client.prompt(promptText);\\n    if (editTimer) {\\n      clearTimeout(editTimer);\\n      editTimer = null;\\n    }\",\"numLines\":100,\"startLine\":519,\"totalLines\":1773}}"</outcome>
</observed_from_primary_session>

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Examined existing draft update mechanism in Telegram bridge</title>
  <subtitle>Current implementation uses coalesced editNowInner with 10s keepalive, mixing all content in single draft channel</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Draft updates flow through editNowInner which tries tryEditRichMessageDraft first, falls back to editMessageText</fact>
    <fact>Coalescing mechanism (coalesceAsyncRunner) prevents concurrent edit calls from creating duplicate placeholder messages</fact>
    <fact>Draft keepalive runs every 10s to refresh ~30s TTL ephemeral drafts during long tool executions</fact>
    <fact>All content (answer text + tool progress) currently renders through same lastRendered state in single draft channel</fact>
    <fact>Self-fusion feature injects alternative perspective from trio-llm for high-stakes decisions during goal mode</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Investigated the current draft update architecture in run-prompt.ts to understand how streaming edits work before implementing the tool progress separation solution. The existing system uses a coalesced async runner pattern where editNowInner handles draft updates, first attempting rich message draft via tryEditRichMessageDraft (when tgRichStreamEnabled), then falling back to standard editMessageText. A critical keepalive mechanism refreshes drafts every 10 seconds to prevent the 30s TTL from expiring during long tool executions. The coalescing layer prevents race conditions where multiple triggers (onUpdate chunks, keepalive, rate-limit recovery) could create duplicate placeholder messages. Currently, all content—both append-only answer text and mutable tool progress—flows through the same lastRendered state and gets mixed into a single draft channel, which is the root cause of the jitter issue identified in the research phase. This understanding is foundational for implementing Solution A (separate status message channel for tool progress) or Solution B (freeze tool format in draft).</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

The user is observing a primary session where someone is investigating the Telegram bridge code. They're looking at the cleanup/finally block to understand how resources are cleaned up after a turn completes.

The grep found the finally block at line 1691, and the subsequent Read shows:
- The finally block cleans up editTimer, draftKeepalive interval, and calls unregisterRecovery()
- There's a turn token validation (session._turnSeq === turnToken) to prevent old turns from interfering with new turns
- Only cleans up shared fields (onUpdate, inflight, inactivity timer) if this turn still owns the session
- Drains pending meta prompts queue after cleanup

This is discovering how the current implementation manages lifecycle and cleanup, which is relevant context for implementing the solution (either A or B) to separate tool progress updates.

  <title>Mapped cleanup and lifecycle management in draft update flow</title>
  <subtitle>Finally block uses turn token validation to prevent old turns from interfering with new turn's streaming</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Finally block clears three resources: editTimer timeout, draftKeepalive interval, and recovery callback via unregisterRecovery()</fact>
    <fact>Turn token validation (session._turnSeq === turnToken) ensures only the current turn cleans up shared session state</fact>
    <fact>Shared state cleared includes onUpdate callback, inflight flag, inactivity timer, and pending meta queue</fact>
    <fact>Pending meta prompts drain asynchronously 500ms after cleanup to avoid blocking turn completion</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Examined the cleanup lifecycle in run-prompt.ts to understand resource management patterns needed for implementing separate status message channel. The finally block demonstrates a token-based ownership pattern where each turn has a unique turnToken that validates ownership before cleaning shared session state. This prevents race conditions where an old turn's cleanup could interfere with a new turn's active streaming. Three critical resources get cleaned: the throttle timer (editTimer), the draft keepalive interval (preventing 30s TTL expiry), and the rate-limit recovery callback. The turn token pattern will be essential when implementing Solution A's separate status message channel—status message cleanup must follow the same ownership validation to avoid deleting status messages that belong to a newer turn.</narrative>

content.thinking:
This is a significant code change. The user is implementing Solution A (OpenClaw-style separation) to fix the Telegram draft jitter issue. Let me analyze what was done:

1. **Modified `renderDraftReply` function** in `telegram-ui.ts`:
   - Removed thinking block and tool lines from draft rendering
   - Simplified to only render: header + body + cursor (pure append-only content)
   - Updated comments to explain the new approach: "官方規格是純 append-only 模型，沒有 mid-text 變動的更新方式"
   - Removed all the statusTail logic that was causing jitter

2. **Added new `renderStatusMessage` function**:
   - Extracts thinking preview and tool progress lines
   - Returns empty string if no status to show (signal to not create message)
   - Will be rendered in a separate status message updated via editMessageText
   - Combines tools and thinking block, ordered as [tools, thinkingBlock]

This is the first step of implementing the separation pattern researched earlier. The volatile content (thinking/tools with emoji flips, timestamp inserts, window sliding) is now separated from the append-only draft content.

This is a feature/refactor - it's implementing a new approach to fix the jitter problem, but it's not complete yet (still need to wire up the status message handling in run-prompt.ts).

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>refactor</type>
  <title>Separated volatile status content from append-only draft rendering</title>
  <subtitle>Split draft into pure body channel and separate status message to eliminate Telegram jitter</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>renderDraftReply now only renders header + body + cursor with pure append-only content</fact>
    <fact>New renderStatusMessage function extracts thinking preview and tool progress into separate renderable content</fact>
    <fact>Status message returns empty string when no status exists, signaling no message creation needed</fact>
    <fact>Tool lines and thinking block removed from draft's statusTail to prevent mid-text changes during streaming</fact>
    <fact>Status message will use editMessageText for updates which redraws without character fade-in animation</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Implemented the first phase of Solution A (OpenClaw-style channel separation) to fix Telegram draft message jitter. The core issue was that Telegram's draft update animation uses append-only semantics—new characters fade in at the end based on length difference, but any mid-text changes (emoji updates, timestamp inserts) cause visible jitter because the spec provides no mid-text update mechanism. The refactor splits renderDraftReply into two rendering paths: (1) renderDraftReply now produces pure append-only content containing only header + body + cursor, ensuring smooth fade-in as answer text grows, and (2) new renderStatusMessage extracts the volatile content (thinking preview, tool progress with dynamic emoji/timing) that was previously mixed into the draft's statusTail. The status message will be sent as a separate Telegram message and updated via editMessageText, which performs whole-message redraw without animation, eliminating jitter from emoji flips and timestamp changes. This addresses the root cause identified in research—mixing mutable and immutable content in the same append-only channel. Next step is wiring the status message lifecycle in run-prompt.ts (send once, edit on updates, delete on turn completion).</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/telegram-ui.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

  <narrative>Added renderStatusMessage to the imports in run-prompt.ts to support the next implementation phase. The function was just created in telegram-ui.ts to extract volatile content (thinking preview, tool progress) from the draft channel. This import wiring prepares run-prompt.ts to call renderStatusMessage and manage a separate status message alongside the draft, completing the OpenClaw-style channel separation pattern that eliminates Telegram draft jitter by keeping mutable content out of the append-only draft channel.</narrative>

}\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Orphan turn flusher ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n//\\n// Delivers agent output that arrived with NO active prompt turn — e.g. a\\n// Claude Code harness Shares the 5-min\\n  // window used by specialist/parallel result injection.\\n  if (isDuplicateResult(chatId, rawText)) {\\n    console.log(`[orphan ${chatId}] skipped (dedup hit)`);\\n    return;\\n  }\\n\\n  const ab7 = transformReplyTokens(rawText, {\\n    parseDuration,\\n    parseAbsoluteTime,\\n    parseRecurrence,\\n    computeNextRunAt,\\n  });\\n\\n  // SCHEDULE tokens — register the same way the normal path does.\\n  const scheduleReceipts: string[] = [];\\n  for (const se of ab7.scheduleTokens) {\\n    try {\\n      const entry = await scheduleStore.add({\\n        chatId,\\n        userId: session.userId ?? chatId,\\n        runAt: se.runAt,\\n        prompt: se.prompt,\\n        recurrence: se.recurrence,\\n      });\\n      scheduler.arm(entry);\\n      const delta = formatRelative(se.runAt - Date.now());\\n      const recurTag = se.recurrence ? ` [${formatRecurrence(se.recurrence)}]` : \\\"\\\";\\n      scheduleReceipts.push(\\n        `⏰ 已排程 ${entry.id}${recurTag}，${delta} 後觸發（${new Date(se.runAt).toLocaleString()}）`,\\n      );\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.error(`[orphan ${chatId}] schedule token register failed:`, err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // Surface (but don't act on) tokens that require live-turn context.\\n  const ignored: string[] = [];\\n  if (ab7.goalDone) ignored.push(\\\"GOAL_DONE\\\");\\n  if (ab7.restartToken) ignored.push(\\\"RESTART\\\");\\n  if (ab7.continueToken) ignored.push(\\\"CONTINUE\\\");\\n  if (ab7.wikiQuery) ignored.push(\\\"WIKI_QUERY\\\");\\n  if (ab7.skillPropose) ignored.push(\\\"SKILL_PROPOSE\\\");\\n  if (ab7.specialistProxy) ignored.push(\\\"SPECIALIST_PROXY\\\");\\n  if (ab7.moaPlan) ignored.push(\\\"MOA_PLAN\\\");\\n  if (ignored.length) {\\n    console.log(`[orphan ${chatId}] ignoring turn-context token(s): ${ignored.join(\\\", \\\")}`);\\n  }\\n\\n  const body = scheduleReceipts.length\\n    ? (ab7.text ? `${ab7.text}\\\\n\\\\n${scheduleReceipts.join(\\\"\\\\n\\\")}` : scheduleReceipts.join(\\\"\\\\n\\\"))\\n    : ab7.text;\\n  const askKeyboard = buildAskKeyboard(ab7.askTokens);\\n\\n  // Text delivery (chunked). Rate-limit aware: queue for replay if throttled.\\n  if (body.trim().length > 0) {\\n    const chunks = splitForTelegram(body);\\n    for (let i = 0; i < chunks.length; i++) {\\n      const isLast = i === chunks.length - 1;\\n      const pageTag = chunks.length > 1 ? `(${i + 1}/${chunks.length})\\\\n` : \\\"\\\";\\n      const text = pageTag + chunks[i];\\n      const extra = isLast && askKeyboard = await bot.api.sendMessage(chatId, text, extra);\\n        if (isLast && askKeyboard && msg?.message_id !== undefined) {\\n          askRegistry.set(msg.message_id, ab7.askTokens);\\n        }\\n      } catch (err) {\\n        console.error(`[orphan ${chatId}] chunk send failed, queueing for replay:`, err);\\n        queuePendingMessage(chatId, text);\\n      }\\n      if (!isLast) await new Promise<void>((r) => setTimeout(r, 1100));\\n    }\\n  } else if (askKeyboard) {\\n    // ASK-only orphan turn (rare) — carry the keyboard on a minimal message.\\n    try {\\n      const msg = await bot.api.sendMessage(chatId, \\\"（背景任務完成）\\\", { reply_markup: askKeyboard });\\n      if (msg?.message_id !== undefined) askRegistry.set(msg.message_id, ab7.askTokens);\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.error(`[orphan ${chatId}] ask-only send failed:`, err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // SEND_FILE — sendFilesByPaths needs a Context-like; build a minimal shim.\\n  if (ab7.sendFiles.length > 0) {\\n    const fakeCtx = {\\n      chat: { id: chatId, type: \\\"private\\\" as const },\\n      replyWithPhoto: (f: unknown, extra?: unknown) => bot.api.sendPhoto(chatId, f as any, extra as any),\\n      replyWithDocument: (f: unknown, extra?: unknown) => bot.api.sendDocument(chatId, f as any, extra as any),\\n    } as unknown as Context;\\n    try {\\n      await sendFilesByPaths(fakeCtx, ab7.sendFiles);\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.error(`[orphan ${chatId}] sendFilesByPaths failed:`, err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // Stickers\\n  for (const emotion of ab7.stickerEmotions) {\\n    const fileId = STICKER_MAP[emotion];\\n    if (!fileId) continue;\\n    try {\\n      await bot.api.sendSticker(chatId, fileId);\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(`[orphan ${chatId}] sticker ${emotion} failed — ${(err as Error).message}`);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  console.log(\\n    `[orphan ${chatId}] delivered (textChars=${body.length}, files=${ab7.sendFiles.length}, ` +\\n      `asks=${ab7.askTokens.length}, schedules=${scheduleReceipts.length}, stickers=${ab7.stickerEmotions.length})`,\\n  );\\n}\\n\\n// ─── runPrompt ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\nexport async function runPrompt(\\n  ctx: Context,\\n  session: ChatSession,\\n  text: string,\\n  mode: RunPromptMode = { kind: \\\"user\\\" },\\n) {\\n  const { bot, sessions, rateLimitedUntil, onRateLimitRecovery, queuePendingMessage,\\n    handleRestart, STREAM_EDIT_INTERVAL_MS, MEMORY_DIR, scheduler, scheduleStore } = content.thinking:
This is a significant implementation step. The code is adding the status message lifecycle management functions to handle the separate status channel for draft mode. Let me break down what was added:

1. **State variables** for the status message:
   - `statusMsgId` - tracks the message ID of the separate status message
   - `lastStatusRendered` - tracks last rendered content to avoid redundant updates

2. **deleteStatusMessage()** - cleanup function that:
   - Deletes the status message when the turn ends
   - Resets state variables
   - Catches errors gracefully (e.g., if user already deleted the message)

3. **updateStatusMessage()** - update function that:
   - Early returns if turn is finalized
   - Renders status content via `renderStatusMessage(session)`
   - Skips update if content is empty or unchanged
   - On first call (statusMsgId undefined): sends new message with `disable_notification: true` (silent)
   - Checks if turn finalized during await and immediately deletes if so
   - Has a critical workaround: after sendMessage, re-sends the draft because "sendMessage (general message) immediately clears the chat's live draft" - this is the gotcha mentioned in research
   - On subsequent calls: uses editMessageText to update in place (no fade animation)
   - Error handling: transient errors don't update lastStatusRendered, so next tick retries

This is the core infrastructure for the OpenClaw-style solution. The functions are defined but not yet wired into the streaming flow or cleanup lifecycle.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Implemented status message lifecycle management for separate volatile content channel</title>
  <subtitle>Added updateStatusMessage and deleteStatusMessage functions to manage tool/thinking progress outside draft</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>statusMsgId and lastStatusRendered state variables track separate status message lifecycle</fact>
    <fact>updateStatusMessage sends initial silent message (disable_notification: true), then uses editMessageText for updates</fact>
    <fact>Critical workaround: after sendMessage creates status message, draft must be re-sent because general messages clear live drafts</fact>
    <fact>deleteStatusMessage handles cleanup with graceful error handling for user-deleted messages</fact>
    <fact>Early-exit guards prevent status updates after turn finalization, with post-await check to delete orphaned messages</fact>
    <fact>Error handling pattern: failed updates preserve lastStatusRendered for automatic retry on next tick</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Implemented the core lifecycle management for the separate status message channel that solves Telegram draft jitter. The updateStatusMessage function handles the two-phase lifecycle: (1) first call sends a new silent message (disable_notification: true) containing tool progress and thinking preview, and (2) subsequent calls use editMessageText to update in place without character fade-in animation. A critical implementation detail addresses a Telegram gotcha discovered during design—sendMessage (regular message) immediately clears the chat's live draft, so after creating the status message, the code must re-send the draft with the same draftId to restore it. The deleteStatusMessage function provides cleanup with graceful error handling for edge cases like user-deleted messages. Turn finalization guards prevent updates after completion, with an additional post-await check that immediately deletes the status message if the turn finalized during the sendMessage call, preventing orphaned status messages. The error handling pattern avoids updating lastStatusRendered on failure, allowing automatic retry on the next tick. These functions complete the infrastructure layer—next step is wiring them into editNow callbacks and finally block cleanup to activate the separate channel pattern.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
This is a critical integration edit that wires the updateStatusMessage function into the draft update flow. Let me understand what changed:

**Before:**
- The draft path had an early return if draftContent === lastRendered
- It would update the draft and immediately return if successful
- If draft failed, it would downgrade to placeholder mode

**After:**
- The early return is removed with a comment explaining why: "draft 內容不變 ≠ 沒事做——tool/thinking 可能有變（status 通道與 draft 通道獨立判斷 identical）"
- The draft update logic is wrapped in `if (draftContent !== lastRendered)` instead of early-returning
- When downgrading to placeholder mode, it now calls `void deleteStatusMessage()` first to avoid double-display
- After the draft update succeeds OR after handling downgrade, there's a new check: "仍在 draft 模式（未降級）→ 更新 status 通道後結束本 tick"
- If still in draft mode, it calls `await updateStatusMessage()` then returns

This is the key integration: every editNow tick now updates BOTH channels independently:
1. Update draft channel (if content changed)
2. Update status channel (always, if still in draft mode and status content changed)

The removal of the early return is crucial - previously, if the draft content was identical, the whole function would bail early. But now that tool/thinking progress is in a separate channel, the status might have changed even if the draft didn't.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Wired status message updates into draft streaming loop</title>
  <subtitle>Draft and status channels now update independently each tick, status updates even when draft content unchanged</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Removed early return when draftContent === lastRendered because tool/thinking in status channel may change independently</fact>
    <fact>Draft update wrapped in if-check instead of early-return, allowing status channel update after draft stabilizes</fact>
    <fact>When degrading to placeholder mode, deleteStatusMessage prevents double-display since placeholder includes tool/thinking</fact>
    <fact>New final check calls updateStatusMessage if still in draft mode, ensuring status channel updates every tick</fact>
    <fact>Two independent identical-content checks: lastRendered for draft, lastStatusRendered for status (in updateStatusMessage)</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Integrated the status message channel into the draft streaming loop, completing the separation pattern. The critical change is removing the early return when draft content is unchanged—previously this would skip the entire editNow tick, but now that tool progress lives in a separate channel, the status might need updating even when answer text is stable. The draft update logic is now wrapped in a conditional check rather than early-returning, and after handling draft (whether updated or degraded), a new final section checks if still in draft mode and calls updateStatusMessage. When degrading from draft to placeholder mode, deleteStatusMessage is called first because the placeholder path's renderReply includes tool/thinking lines, preventing double-display. This creates two independent update loops: draft channel checks draftContent !== lastRendered, status channel checks its own content in updateStatusMessage. The system now updates both channels each tick, with each channel's identical-content check independent—the append-only draft stays smooth while the status message redraws volatile content without jitter.</narrative>

  </facts>
  <narrative>Completed the OpenClaw-style channel separation implementation by adding status message cleanup to the finally block. The deleteStatusMessage call is placed after draft keepalive cleanup and before unregisterRecovery, ensuring the temporary status message is removed on every turn exit path—normal completion, cancellation, supersession, or error. Using void (fire-and-forget) ensures cleanup never blocks turn finalization. The comment clarifies why cleanup is unconditional: the final message includes toolsSuffix with complete tool summaries, making the temporary status message redundant once the turn completes. This completes the full lifecycle: (1) renderStatusMessage produces volatile content, (2) updateStatusMessage manages send-once then edit-in-place lifecycle during streaming, (3) deleteStatusMessage removes it on turn completion. The implementation solves the original Telegram draft jitter problem by keeping append-only answer text in the draft channel (smooth fade-in) while mutable tool/thinking progress lives in a separate editMessageText channel (whole-message redraw, no animation, no jitter).</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>

  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Examined existing draft render test expectations</title>
  <subtitle>Tests verify old behavior with tools/thinking in draft, now needs updates for separated status channel</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Test 2 expects tools section in draft frame and verifies body prefix stability when tools increase</fact>
    <fact>Test 3 verifies body append creates longer common prefix when tools/thinking unchanged</fact>
    <fact>Tests check markdown preservation, empty-string handling, long-body truncation, and token stripping</fact>
    <fact>All tests use renderDraftReply expecting tools/thinking in output, but new implementation moved those to renderStatusMessage</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Examined the existing test file check-draft-render.mjs which validates draft rendering behavior. The tests were written for the old implementation where tools and thinking appeared in the draft frame along with body content. Test 2 specifically verifies that when tools increase, the body+cursor prefix remains stable (avoiding head insertion jitter). Test 3 checks that body appends create a longer common prefix when tools/thinking are unchanged. However, the new status message separation fundamentally changes these expectations—renderDraftReply now outputs only header + body + cursor, with tools and thinking extracted to renderStatusMessage. These tests will need updating to reflect the new architecture: tests for renderDraftReply should verify pure append-only behavior without any tools/thinking, and new tests for renderStatusMessage should verify the independent status channel rendering.</narrative>

    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>

    <file>scripts/check-draft-render.mjs</file>

</observation>

content.thinking: The user is updating the test file to import renderStatusMessage so they can test the new separate status message channel. This is a necessary step to update the tests to reflect the new architecture where tools and thinking are extracted from the draft and rendered separately.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>change</type>
  <title>Added renderStatusMessage import to draft render tests</title>
  <subtitle>Preparing to update tests for separated status message channel architecture</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>renderStatusMessage imported alongside renderDraftReply in check-draft-render.mjs</fact>
    <fact>Tests will need updates to verify new split-channel behavior instead of old unified draft rendering</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Added renderStatusMessage to the test imports in preparation for updating the draft render smoke tests to reflect the new architecture. The existing tests validate the old behavior where tools and thinking appeared in the draft frame alongside body content. With the channel separation implementation complete, these tests need restructuring: renderDraftReply tests should verify pure append-only body+cursor output, while new renderStatusMessage tests should verify independent status channel rendering of tools and thinking progress. This import is the first step toward adapting the test suite to match the implemented OpenClaw-style separation pattern.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>scripts/check-draft-render.mjs</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
This is a significant test update that reflects the new architecture where draft and status are separated. Let me break down what changed:

1. Test 1 now verifies that draft frames are "pure" - they don't contain thinking or tools anymore, just body content
2. Test 2 is the critical one - it now tests that tools/thinking changes have ZERO effect on the draft frame (this is the core fix for jitter)
3. Test 2b is new - tests renderStatusMessage separately to verify it contains tools and thinking
4. Test 2c is new - tests the replyHeader feature

This is a great update that validates the channel separation implementation actually works as designed.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Updated tests to validate separated draft and status message channels</title>
  <subtitle>New tests verify draft purity and independence from volatile tool/thinking changes, solving jitter</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Test 1 now verifies draft frames contain only body, asserting absence of thinking (💭) and tool markers (✅)</fact>
    <fact>Test 2 proves core jitter fix: tools/thinking changes produce identical draft frames (frame1 === frame2)</fact>
    <fact>New test 2b validates renderStatusMessage independently contains tool lines and thinking preview</fact>
    <fact>New test 2c validates replyHeader behavior appears in draft even when body is empty</fact>
    <fact>Tests verify tool names show only kind without parameters (Read not Read File) and timing display (2.7s)</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Completely rewrote the first two tests to validate the new channel separation architecture that eliminates Telegram draft jitter. Test 1 now verifies "draft purity"—renderDraftReply output contains body content but explicitly excludes thinking and tool markers, proving volatile content has been successfully extracted. Test 2 demonstrates the core jitter fix: when the same body is rendered with different tools/thinking state, the draft frames are bitwise identical (frame1 === frame2), proving that volatile changes have zero impact on the draft channel. This is the append-only guarantee that prevents Telegram's character fade-in animation from causing visual jitter. New test 2b independently validates renderStatusMessage contains the extracted volatile content—completed tools with timing, running tools with spinner emoji, thinking preview, and proper empty-string handling. New test 2c validates replyHeader support. These tests prove the implementation matches the OpenClaw pattern: draft channel is purely append-only (smooth animation), status channel carries mutable content (whole-message redraw, no animation, no jitter).</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>scripts/check-draft-render.mjs</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

  <narrative>All four smoke test suites pass cleanly after implementing the OpenClaw-style status message channel separation, confirming the solution is complete and working correctly. The draft render tests (16 passing) validate the core fix: draft frames now contain only pure append-only body content, completely independent from volatile tool/thinking changes. The key test proves frame1 === frame2 when only tools/thinking change—this guarantees Telegram's append-only animation semantics won't cause jitter. The renderStatusMessage tests validate the extracted volatile content displays correctly in the separate channel. Three additional test suites (31 more tests) confirm no regressions in token stripping, concurrent edit handling, or goal continuation logic. The implementation successfully solves the Telegram draft jitter problem by splitting mutable content (tool progress, thinking preview) into a separate editMessageText channel that redraws without animation, while keeping the draft channel purely append-only for smooth character fade-in.</narrative>

  <narrative>Successfully committed the complete dual-channel draft streaming implementation that eliminates Telegram draft jitter. The commit message comprehensively documents the root cause (mid-text changes in append-only animation cause jitter), the architecture (dual independent channels), and the implementation details including critical gotchas like sendMessage clearing live drafts.
